### 3.1.3 Atomic Number and Mass Number

What makes the atoms of one element different from another is the number of protons present in the nucleus. This is called the *atomic number*. For example, atoms of hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen and so on are primarily different because they each contain different numbers of protons.

## Definition

The *atomic number* is the number of protons present in the nucleus of an atom. It is the defining property that distinguishes the atoms of one element from another.

## Core Principles

- The identity of a chemical element is determined by the number of protons in its nucleus (its atomic number).
- Atoms of different elements (e.g., hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen) are primarily different because they contain different numbers of protons.

## Sub-concepts

- Atomic number (proton count in the nucleus)
- Mass number (referenced in the section title as a related quantity)

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Used to identify and distinguish chemical elements such as hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen by their proton counts.
